profile.summary: Leads the product function at a director level, setting product strategy, portfolio priorities, and operating rhythm across multiple teams or a business area. Owns roadmap tradeoffs, product org health, and alignment with engineering, design, go-to-market, finance, and executives. Usually manages product leaders or senior PMs and is accountable for outcomes, not just shipping.

known_challenges.text: Balancing long-term product strategy with short-term revenue, customer, and executive pressure.
Keeping multiple teams aligned on priorities when dependencies and tradeoffs cut across the portfolio.
Shifting from direct product problem-solving to coaching leaders and scaling decision quality through others.
Carrying visible accountability for product outcomes without always controlling engineering capacity, market timing, or sales commitments.
Building a consistent product operating model across teams with different maturity, rituals, and standards.
Managing context switching across strategy, people leadership, stakeholder management, and escalations.

recommended_question_themes.why: This role is expected to make hard calls across products and teams, so weak priority signals create churn everywhere.
A Head of Product succeeds through other leaders, making coaching depth and succession strength central to the role.
Misalignment with the leadership team can stall strategy, distort roadmaps, and create avoidable rework.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Product operating model and team consistency
recommended_question_themes.why: At this level, uneven planning, discovery, and delivery habits across teams become a scaling problem.
The role often absorbs conflict, ambiguity, and urgent asks, which can quietly erode judgment and energy.
Strategy quality depends on whether this leader is getting clear input from customers, data, and commercial teams.

terminology.meaning: The long-term direction and choices for the product.
Planned sequence of product bets and delivery over time.
The set of products, teams, or initiatives under management.
Objectives and key results used to track goals.
Evidence the product solves a real market need.
Work to understand problems before building solutions.
How a product is launched, sold, and adopted.
Primary measure of product value or progress.
Aligning people with different goals and influence.
Deciding what gets focus and what does not.

listen_for: Whether they talk in terms of business outcomes or only roadmap output.
How often they mention coaching product leaders versus stepping in personally.
Repeated references to executive churn, last-minute priority changes, or sales-driven overrides.
Whether customer evidence and data are used to justify choices or absent from the story.
Signs they are the bottleneck for decisions, escalations, or cross-team alignment.
avoid: Treating them like a senior individual PM instead of a leader accountable through other product managers.
Assuming roadmap delivery alone is the main measure of success.
Pushing for more hands-on involvement when the real need is stronger delegation and leadership leverage.
Reducing product disagreements to communication issues when they are really strategy or priority conflicts.
